title: Clonal Hematopoiesis and Risk of Atherosclerotic Cardiovascular Disease
journal: The New England Journal of Medicine
research_question: Does clonal hematopoiesis of indeterminate potential (CHIP) contribute causally to atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease?
methods_summary: Whole-exome sequencing to detect CHIP in peripheral-blood cells from four case–control studies (4726 coronary heart disease cases, 3529 controls)
Nested case–control analysis from two prospective cohorts (BioImage and Malmö Diet and Cancer) and two retrospective case–control studies (ATVB and PROMIS) for early-onset myocardial infarction
Gene-level analysis for DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1, and JAK2 mutations
Assessment of coronary-artery calcification via cardiac CT in BioImage participants

Murine model: bone marrow transplantation from Tet2 knockout (homozygous or heterozygous) mice into atherosclerosis-prone Ldlr knockout mice, followed by high-cholesterol diet and lesion quantification
Gene-expression analysis of macrophages from Tet2 knockout mice exposed to LDL
dataset: Four case–control studies: BioImage, Malmö Diet and Cancer (MDC), Atherosclerosis, Thrombosis, and Vascular Biology Italian Study Group (ATVB), and Pakistan Risk of Myocardial Infarction Study (PROMIS); plus three prospective cohorts (Jackson Heart Study, FUSION, Framingham Heart Study) for gene-level analysis
key_findings: CHIP carriers had 1.9 times the risk of coronary heart disease (95% CI, 1.4 to 2.7) in prospective cohorts and 4.0 times the risk of early-onset myocardial infarction (95% CI, 2.4 to 6.7) in retrospective studies
Mutations in DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1, and JAK2 were individually associated with coronary heart disease; JAK2 V617F conferred 12.1 times the risk
CHIP carriers had higher coronary-artery calcification scores; those with variant allele fraction ≥10% had 12 times the risk of high calcification
Mice engrafted with Tet2 knockout bone marrow developed larger atherosclerotic lesions in aortic root and descending aorta compared with controls

Tet2 knockout macrophages showed elevated expression of CXC chemokines (e.g., Cxcl1, Cxcl2, Cxcl3) and proinflammatory cytokines (Il1b, Il6); humans with TET2 mutations had higher plasma interleukin-8 levels
extracted_insights: CHIP, defined by somatic mutations in hematopoietic cells (e.g., DNMT3A, TET2, ASXL1, JAK2), is associated with increased risk of coronary heart disease and early-onset myocardial infarction, likely through a causal mechanism involving enhanced macrophage inflammation and chemokine expression, as demonstrated by Tet2 loss in murine models.
